title: The Impact of Polyphenols on Intestinal Permeability and Barrier Functions. Accumulating research has reported that grape polyphenols can offer protective effects on intestinal barrier functions, possibly via antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, and also promote beneficial gut microbes. For instance, researchers showed that a diet containing 1 percent grape polyphenol extracts rich in B-type proanthocyanidin compounds increased the relative abundance of fecal and cecal Akkermansia muciniphila in mice. Akkermansia muciniphila is known to stimulate intestinal mucin thickness to improve gut barrier integrity. Another group examined the effects of a diet containing 8 percent grape seed meal, which is rich in bioactive compounds, including polyphenols, polyunsaturated fatty acids, and fiber, on the markers of colonic epithelial integrity, mucosal barrier function, and inflammation in dextran sulfate sodium-treated piglets. These investigators observed that the grape seed diet restored epithelial integrity and mucosal barrier reinforcement through the modulation of tight junction genes and proteins, including claudins, occludins, and zonula-1, and tight junction regulators, such as myosin IXB and protein tyrosine phosphatase, as well as mucin-2 gene. The grape seed meal also decreased pro-inflammatory matrix metalloproteinase-2 and matrix metalloproteinase-9 expression and suppressed Toll-like receptor-2 and Toll-like receptor-4 genes. Further, the supplementation of the grape polyphenol extract decreased the ileal gene expression of the lipopolysaccharide-binding protein, an acute-phase protein that promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, suggesting reduced lipopolysaccharide in the circulation and protection of gut barrier integrity. Moreover, researchers reported that the supplementation of grape seed procyanidins decreased the serum concentrations of diamine oxidase, D-lactic acid, and endotoxin in geese, indicating the protection of intestine barrier integrity, and increased the total antioxidant capacity and superoxide dismutase and catalase activity in cecal mucosa. In addition, grape seed proanthocyanidin extract markedly suppressed the dextran sulfate sodium-induced elevation of the T N F-alpha, I L-1 beta, and messenger ribonucleic acid of N L R P 3 while upregulating the messenger ribonucleic acids of tight junction proteins, including zonula occludens-1, occludins, and claudin-1, suggesting the potential improvement of mucosa integrity.

sentences.text: The Impact of Polyphenols on Intestinal Permeability and Barrier Functions.

Accumulating research has reported that grape polyphenols can offer protective effects on intestinal barrier functions, possibly via antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, and also promote beneficial gut microbes.

For instance, researchers showed that a diet containing 1 percent grape polyphenol extracts rich in B-type proanthocyanidin compounds increased the relative abundance of fecal and cecal Akkermansia muciniphila in mice.
Akkermansia muciniphila is known to stimulate intestinal mucin thickness to improve gut barrier integrity.

Another group examined the effects of a diet containing 8 percent grape seed meal, which is rich in bioactive compounds, including polyphenols, polyunsaturated fatty acids, and fiber, on the markers of colonic epithelial integrity, mucosal barrier function, and inflammation in dextran sulfate sodium-treated piglets.

These investigators observed that the grape seed diet restored epithelial integrity and mucosal barrier reinforcement through the modulation of tight junction genes and proteins, including claudins, occludins, and zonula-1, and tight junction regulators, such as myosin IXB and protein tyrosine phosphatase, as well as mucin-2 gene.
The grape seed meal also decreased pro-inflammatory matrix metalloproteinase-2 and matrix metalloproteinase-9 expression and suppressed Toll-like receptor-2 and Toll-like receptor-4 genes.

Further, the supplementation of the grape polyphenol extract decreased the ileal gene expression of the lipopolysaccharide-binding protein, an acute-phase protein that promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, suggesting reduced lipopolysaccharide in the circulation and protection of gut barrier integrity.

Moreover, researchers reported that the supplementation of grape seed procyanidins decreased the serum concentrations of diamine oxidase, D-lactic acid, and endotoxin in geese, indicating the protection of intestine barrier integrity, and increased the total antioxidant capacity and superoxide dismutase and catalase activity in cecal mucosa.

In addition, grape seed proanthocyanidin extract markedly suppressed the dextran sulfate sodium-induced elevation of the T N F-alpha, I L-1 beta, and messenger ribonucleic acid of N L R P 3 while upregulating the messenger ribonucleic acids of tight junction proteins, including zonula occludens-1, occludins, and claudin-1, suggesting the potential improvement of mucosa integrity.
Consistent with the above-cited studies in animals, the protection of the colonic epithelial barrier by grape products has also been evaluated in relevant cellular models.

For instance, a proanthocyanidin-rich grape seed extract was shown to increase the expression of tight junction proteins, elevate the expression of anti-inflammatory cytokines, and decrease pro-inflammatory cytokine gene expression in lipopolysaccharide-treated Caco-2 cells.

Moreover, a grape seed extract suppressed the repeated water avoidance stress-induced increase in colonic concentrations of I L-6, Toll-like receptor 4, and claudin-2 in a rat irritable bowel syndrome model and mitigated I L-6 and I L-1 beta-caused elevation of paracellular permeability in Caco-2 cell monolayers.
In addition, resveratrol has been found to counteract barrier dysfunction induced by Campylobacter jejuni, a bacterial human pathogen known to cause gastroenteritis and irritable bowel syndrome.

Specifically, researchers observed that in human colonic HT-29/B6 cell monolayers, Campylobacter jejuni infection resulted in decreased transepithelial electrical resistance and increased paracellular marker flux of fluorescein.

Concomitantly, Campylobacter jejuni infection also compromised the distribution of the tight junction proteins occludin and claudin-5, which was accompanied by an increased induction of epithelial apoptosis.

Importantly, resveratrol restored Campylobacter jejuni-induced tight junction delocalization of occludin and claudin-5 in the paracellular domain of HT-29/B6 cells, improved the disturbed epithelial barrier, and attenuated the rate of epithelial apoptosis in Campylobacter jejuni-infected I L-10 knockout mice.
In addition to studies in cells and animal models, the potential impact of polyphenols on intestinal permeability has also been examined in humans.

Specifically, in a randomized, controlled, crossover intervention trial, 66 subjects aged 60 years or older with increased intestinal permeability based on serum zonulin levels, a surrogate marker of intestinal permeability, were randomly allocated to one of the two arms of the intervention, consisting of a control diet versus a polyphenol-rich diet.
After an 8-week intervention, participants with a polyphenol-rich diet had reduced serum zonulin levels and diastolic blood pressure, which were strongest in those not using antihypertensive drugs.
Interestingly, the intervention led to a significant increase in fiber-fermenting and butyrate-producing bacteria, such as Ruminococcaceae and members of the genus Faecalibacterium.

In summary, studies in cells, animals, and humans have consistently shown that grape products are protective toward intestinal barrier functions and prevent leaky gut induced by pathogen infection, lipopolysaccharide, stress, and pro-inflammatory cytokines.
These activities likely contribute to the disease-preventing effects of grape polyphenols, which are discussed below. below
